engineGuide.h2: Volkswagen Golf Engine Codes - Full Technical Specifications & Replacement Guide
engineGuide.h3: Every engine code fitted to the Volkswagen Golf across all generations, with technical specs, compatible variants and trims, known failure points, and average rebuilt prices from UK specialists. Select any engine code to compare quotes.

entries.title: 1.6L Diesel
entries.history: The workhorse 1.6 TDI for Mk6/Mk7 Golf; high-volume but plagued by injector leak-off failures driving UK replacement demand.
compatibleVariants: 1.6 TDI S, 1.6 TDI Match, 1.6 TDI SE, 1.6 TDI BlueMotion (Mk6/Mk7)
commonFailures: Injector leak-off pipe seal failure causing oil dilution (70k-120k miles)
DPF clogging on short-journey urban use triggering limp mode
EGR cooler cracking leading to coolant loss and overheating
entries.cta: Get quotes for CAYC Volkswagen Golf engine replacement

entries.title: 1.6L Diesel
entries.history: Lower-output variant of the 1.6 TDI, fitted to entry-level trims; shares CAYC architecture and failure modes.
commonFailures: Same injector leak-off vulnerability as CAYC
Swirl flap actuator wear affecting cold-start performance
DPF regeneration failure on frequent short trips
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRBC Volkswagen Golf engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel
entries.history: The mainstream 2.0 TDI for Mk6/Mk7 Golf; robust but timing chain tensioner design flaw created high replacement demand.
compatibleVariants: 2.0 TDI S, 2.0 TDI Match, 2.0 TDI SE (Mk6/Mk7)
commonFailures: Hydraulic timing chain tensioner failure causing chain jump (80k-140k miles)
Oil pump drive wear leading to low oil pressure warnings
DPF/EGR blockage on urban-use vehicles
entries.cta: Get quotes for CFHC Volkswagen Golf engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel 150hp
entries.history: Updated 2.0 TDI with 150hp output for Mk7 Golf; improved emissions but retained timing component vulnerabilities.
compatibleVariants: 2.0 TDI SE, 2.0 TDI Match, pre-facelift GTD (Mk7)
commonFailures: Timing chain tensioner wear (less severe than CFHC but still monitor)
Turbo actuator sticking causing boost control faults
DPF differential pressure sensor failures
entries.cta: Get quotes for CUNA Volkswagen Golf engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Diesel 184hp GTD
entries.history: High-output twin-turbo 2.0 TDI for the GTD performance diesel; delivers strong torque but complex turbo system adds failure points.
commonFailures: Twin-turbo oil seal leaks causing blue smoke and oil consumption
EGR cooler cracking under high thermal load
DPF ash accumulation requiring professional cleaning
entries.cta: Get quotes for CRLB Volkswagen Golf GTD engine

entries.title: 1.9L Diesel PD
entries.history: The legendary 1.9 PD TDI powered Mk4/Mk5 Golfs; renowned for longevity but injector and cam wear emerge at high mileage.
compatibleVariants: 1.9 TDI S, 1.9 TDI Match, 1.9 TDI SE (Mk4/Mk5)
commonFailures: PD injector failure causing rough running and smoke
Camshaft lobe wear on high-mileage units (>150k miles)
Dual-mass flywheel rattle and clutch wear
entries.cta: Get quotes for BKC Volkswagen Golf 1.9 TDI engine

entries.title: 1.4L Petrol 122hp
entries.history: The mainstream 1.4 TSI for early Mk7 Golf; EA211 architecture improved on EA111 but timing belt tensioner remains a watchpoint.
compatibleVariants: 1.4 TSI S, 1.4 TSI Match, 1.4 TSI SE (Mk7)
commonFailures: Timing belt tensioner wear requiring preventative replacement (~80k miles)
Carbon buildup on intake valves affecting idle and throttle response
Turbo wastegate actuator rattle causing boost control faults
entries.cta: Get quotes for CMBA Volkswagen Golf 1.4 TSI engine

entries.title: 1.4L Petrol 125hp ACT
entries.history: Updated 1.4 TSI with cylinder deactivation (ACT) for improved economy; generally reliable but ACT solenoid faults emerge.
compatibleVariants: 1.4 TSI ACT SE, 1.4 TSI ACT Match (Mk7/Mk7.5)
commonFailures: ACT solenoid faults causing cylinder deactivation errors
Turbo wastegate rattle under part-throttle conditions
Carbon buildup on intake valves (direct injection)
entries.cta: Get quotes for CZDA Volkswagen Golf engine replacement

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol 190/220hp
entries.history: The EA888 Gen 3 2.0 TSI for Mk7 GTI; significant reliability improvements over Gen 2 but timing components still require monitoring.
commonFailures: Timing chain tensioner wear (less severe than Gen 2 but monitor for rattle)
High-pressure fuel pump cam follower wear
Turbo oil feed line coking on hard-driven examples
entries.cta: Get quotes for CXCA Volkswagen Golf GTI engine

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol 220/245hp
entries.history: High-output EA888 Gen 3 for facelift Mk7 GTI; further refinements but early units saw piston ring land concerns under hard use.
commonFailures: Piston ring land failure on early Gen 3 units under hard use/tuning
Turbo bearing wear on high-mileage or tuned examples
Timing chain tensioner monitoring still recommended
entries.cta: Get quotes for CXDA Volkswagen Golf GTI engine

entries.title: 2.0L Petrol 280/300hp R
entries.history: The high-output EA888 for Golf R; delivers serious performance but turbo and oil system demands require meticulous maintenance.
commonFailures: Turbocharger bearing wear on high-mileage or track-used examples
Intercooler condensation causing hydrolock risk in cold climates
Oil feed line coking under hard driving conditions
entries.cta: Get quotes for CJX Volkswagen Golf R engine specialist

entries.title: 1.4L Petrol 122hp (EA111)
entries.history: Early 1.4 TSI with twincharger (supercharger+turbo) on select Mk6 Golfs; complex but powerful, with timing chain as key vulnerability.
commonFailures: EA111 timing chain tensioner failure causing chain jump (70k-110k miles)
Carbon buildup on intake valves affecting performance
High-pressure fuel pump cam follower wear
entries.cta: Get quotes for CAXA Volkswagen Golf 1.4 TSI engine

entries.title: 1.4L Petrol PHEV
entries.history: The plug-in hybrid powertrain for Golf GTE; combines 1.4 TSI with electric motor for electric-only urban driving and extended range.
commonFailures: High-voltage battery capacity degradation reducing electric range
Electric motor bearing wear on high-mileage examples
DSG mechatronic unit faults affecting hybrid mode engagement
entries.cta: Enquire about CXA Volkswagen Golf GTE hybrid engine availability

commonProblems.h2: Volkswagen Golf Engine Problems - What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: The Volkswagen Golf's most common engine issues centre on the 1.6/2.0 TDI diesel injectors and DPF systems, and the EA888/E211 petrol timing chain failures. Every rebuilt engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components designed to address the original failure point - plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Use this decision tool to compare repair vs replacement costs for your specific Golf variant.

problems.h4: 1.6 TDI Injector Failure - Why CAYC/CRBC Engines Fail
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk6/Mk7 1.6 TDI (2009-2017, CAYC, CRBC, CRKA)
problems.rootCause: Injector leak-off pipe seals degrade under heat cycling, causing diesel to wash into cylinders and dilute oil, leading to bearing wear.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace leak-off pipes and copper seals, clean injector seats
repairOptions.longevity: Viable only if caught early before oil dilution damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit four new/remanufactured injectors with coding
repairOptions.longevity: Restores performance but doesn't address underlying DPF strain

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt unit with upgraded injectors, DPF-cleaned, bench-tested
repairOptions.longevity: Best value for high-mileage Golfs; addresses root cause with warranty

problems.recommendation: On a Mk6/Mk7 Golf worth £3,000-£6,000, injector seal replacement is cost-effective if the engine shows no oil dilution symptoms. If the car has exceeded 100k miles or shows bearing knock, a rebuilt CAYC engine with upgraded injectors offers better long-term value and peace of mind.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt CAYC engine prices for your Volkswagen Golf

problems.h4: 2.0 TDI Timing Chain Tensioner Failure - CFHC/CUNA Risk
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk6/Mk7 2.0 TDI (2009-2017, CFHC, CUNA, CRLB)
problems.rootCause: Hydraulic tensioner loses pressure over time, allowing chain to slacken and jump teeth, causing catastrophic valve/piston contact.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace tensioner, guide rails, and chain if accessible
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable before rattling noise appears; requires early diagnosis

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove head, replace bent valves, machine surface, reassemble
repairOptions.longevity: High labour cost; success depends on extent of piston damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt unit with updated tensioner design, upgraded guides, bench-tested
repairOptions.longevity: Most reliable solution; eliminates recurrence risk with warranty coverage

problems.recommendation: If your 2.0 TDI Golf develops a marbles-in-a-can rattle on cold start, stop driving immediately. For cars under 100k miles with no damage, preventative tensioner replacement may suffice. For higher mileage or confirmed interference damage, a rebuilt CFHC/CUNA engine is the only cost-effective path back on the road.
problems.cta: Get quotes for CFHC Volkswagen Golf 2.0 TDI replacement

problems.h4: EA888 Timing Chain Stretch - GTI & R Engine Weakness
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk6/Mk7 GTI/R (2009-2020, CXCA, CXDA, CJX, CHHA)
problems.rootCause: Early EA888 Gen 2 tensioner design allows chain to stretch; Gen 3 improved but still vulnerable under hard use or poor oil maintenance.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace chain, tensioner, guides, and sprockets with updated parts
repairOptions.longevity: Recommended for Gen 2 EA888 at 80k miles before symptoms appear

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove head, replace bent valves, machine, reassemble with new gaskets
repairOptions.longevity: High labour; only viable if pistons undamaged and block intact

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt EA888 with Gen 3+ tensioner, forged internals optional, dyno-tested
repairOptions.longevity: Best for GTI/R owners; restores performance with warranty and upgraded reliability

problems.recommendation: For Mk6 GTI/R with early EA888 Gen 2, preventative chain replacement at 80k miles is essential. If interference damage has occurred, a rebuilt CXDA/CJX engine with Gen 3+ tensioner and upgraded internals offers the only reliable, warrantied solution for performance Golf owners.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt EA888 engine prices for your Volkswagen Golf GTI

problems.h4: DPF & EGR Blockage - Diesel Golf Limp Mode
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk6/Mk7 1.6/2.0 TDI (2009-2020, all EA288 diesels)
problems.rootCause: Frequent short trips prevent DPF regeneration; soot accumulates, EGR clogs, causing restricted exhaust flow, high backpressure, and turbo strain.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Diagnostic forced regen cycle, remove and clean EGR valve and cooler
repairOptions.longevity: Only works if DPF not fully blocked; temporary fix for urban-use Golfs

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit new/remanufactured DPF with gaskets and sensors
repairOptions.longevity: Addresses symptom but not root cause; may recur without driving habit change

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine + DPF Service
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt diesel engine with cleaned/replaced DPF, EGR blanked if desired
repairOptions.longevity: Most comprehensive solution; eliminates combined failure risk with warranty

problems.recommendation: If your Golf TDI shows repeated DPF warnings and limp mode, assess your typical journey patterns. For predominantly urban use, a rebuilt engine with EGR blanking option and DPF service offers the most reliable long-term fix. For mixed/highway drivers, DPF replacement plus driving habit adjustment may suffice.
problems.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf TDI engine replacement with DPF service

problems.h4: 1.4 TSI Timing Chain Stretch - EA211/EA111 Petrol Issue
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf Mk6/Mk7 1.4 TSI (2009-2020, CMBA, CZDA, CAXA)
problems.rootCause: EA111 chain tensioner design flaw allows slack; EA211 improved but still vulnerable to oil starvation or poor maintenance.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace chain, tensioner, guides with updated parts; requires timing cover removal
repairOptions.longevity: Recommended for EA111 at 80k miles; EA211 less urgent but monitor for rattle

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Remove head, replace bent valves, machine surface, reassemble
repairOptions.longevity: High labour cost; only viable if pistons undamaged and block intact

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt unit with updated tensioner design, bench-tested, warranty included
repairOptions.longevity: Best value for high-mileage 1.4 TSI Golfs; eliminates recurrence risk

problems.recommendation: If your 1.4 TSI Golf develops cold-start rattling, stop driving immediately to avoid interference damage. For preventative maintenance on EA111 engines, chain kit replacement at 80k miles is cost-effective. If damage has occurred, a rebuilt CMBA/CZDA engine offers the only reliable, warrantied solution.
problems.cta: Compare rebuilt 1.4 TSI engine prices for your Volkswagen Golf

problems.h4: Turbocharger Failure - All Golf TDI & TSI Engines
problems.affectedModels: Volkswagen Golf all turbocharged variants (2004-Present, all EA111/EA211/EA288/EA888)
problems.rootCause: Oil coking in turbo feed lines, bearing wear from heat cycles, or foreign object damage from failed DPF/EGR components.

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Replace faulty actuator, vacuum lines, or boost sensor; clean vanes if accessible
repairOptions.longevity: Only viable for electronic/control faults, not mechanical turbo damage

repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Supply and fit new/remanufactured turbo with gaskets and oil feed line
repairOptions.longevity: Addresses symptom but doesn't fix underlying oil contamination or DPF strain

repairOptions.tier: Full Replacement - Rebuilt Engine + Turbo Service
repairOptions.whatItInvolves: Complete rebuilt engine with cleaned/replaced turbo, upgraded oil feed, bench-tested
repairOptions.longevity: Most reliable solution; eliminates combined failure risk with warranty coverage

problems.recommendation: If your Golf shows blue smoke, loss of power, or turbo whistle, diagnose whether the turbo failed independently or due to engine oil contamination. For high-mileage Golfs with oil consumption history, a rebuilt engine with upgraded turbo and oil feed offers the most reliable, warrantied path forward.
problems.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf turbo engine replacement with upgraded components

finalCta.h4: Don't Let Engine Failure Write Off Your Volkswagen Golf
finalCta.paragraph: From injector blow-offs to timing chain failures, Volkswagen Golf engine problems are well-documented but rarely mean the end of the road. EngineMarket connects you with trusted UK specialists who supply rebuilt, reconditioned and used Golf engines - all with upgraded components addressing the original failure point and a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty. Compare prices, check supply & fit availability, and get back on the road without the main dealer premium.
finalCta.buttonText: Compare Volkswagen Golf Engine Replacement Prices Now →
finalCta.disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data and repair requests from our network of engine specialists. Dealer pricing is estimated based on published main dealer labour rates and genuine OEM parts pricing. Specialist pricing reflects actual quote data from EngineMarket's UK network. Actual costs vary depending on damage severity, vehicle condition, variant, and workshop location within the UK. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes for your specific Volkswagen Golf and engine code. Every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty - always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier.

engineSizes.h2: Volkswagen Golf Engines by Size - Find the Right Displacement for Your Variant
engineSizes.intro: Every displacement fitted to the Volkswagen Golf across all UK generations is listed below, with compatible engine codes, production years and common failure notes to help you identify the right replacement.

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6L TDI was the backbone of the Mk6 and early Mk7 Golf diesel range, offering strong economy and adequate performance for everyday driving. Widely fitted to Match, SE and BlueMotion trims, it's also the engine most associated with injector leak-off failures in UK Golfs.
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.9L Diesel Engines
items.description: The legendary 1.9L PD TDI powered the Mk4 and early Mk5 Golf, earning a reputation for robustness and high-mileage capability. Still sought after by budget-conscious owners, though injector and camshaft wear become concerns beyond 150k miles.
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf 1.9L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0L TDI became the premium diesel option from Mk5 onwards, offering stronger torque for motorway cruising and GTD performance variants. EA288 updates improved emissions but introduced new failure modes around timing components and DPF systems.
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The downsized 1.0L TSI three-cylinder arrived in the Mk7.5 and continues in the Mk8, offering strong economy and adequate performance for urban driving. Early units saw HPFP and turbo reliability concerns, largely addressed in later production.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L TSI bridged the gap between entry-level and mainstream petrol Golfs from Mk6 to early Mk7. Generally reliable but shares EA111 timing chain vulnerability with larger TSI units.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4L TSI was the mainstream petrol engine across Mk6 and Mk7, available in naturally aspirated and turbocharged forms. EA211 updates improved reliability but timing belt tensioner and carbon buildup remain considerations.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L TSI Evo replaced the 1.4L in late Mk7 and Mk8 Golfs, introducing cylinder deactivation (ACT) for improved economy. Generally reliable but early units saw turbo and HPFP concerns.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8L TSI served as the performance petrol option in select Mk6 Golfs and certain export markets. Shares EA888 architecture with 2.0L units, inheriting timing chain and HPFP vulnerabilities.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L TSI is the heart of the GTI and R performance range, delivering strong power and tuning potential. EA888 Gen 3+ updates significantly improved reliability over early Gen 2 units, but timing components and oil maintenance remain critical.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 3.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 3.2L VR6 powered the iconic R32 Golfs, offering smooth power and distinctive character. A robust engine but timing chain guide wear and oil consumption become concerns on high-mileage examples.
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf 3.2L petrol engine prices

groups.title: Volkswagen Golf Hybrid / Electrified Engine Sizes

items.title: Volkswagen Golf 1.4L Hybrid Engines
items.description: The 1.4L TSI-based plug-in hybrid system powers the GTE and eHybrid variants, combining petrol efficiency with electric-only range. Replacement considerations include both combustion and electric motor components.
items.cta: Enquire about Volkswagen Golf 1.4L hybrid engine availability

fuelTypes.h2: Volkswagen Golf Engines by Fuel Type
fuelTypes.intro: Choose your Volkswagen Golf engine by fuel type to compare realistic replacement prices, known issues, and compatible UK supply options - from early 1.9 TDI to the latest 1.5 TSI Evo and GTE hybrid.

items.title: Volkswagen Golf Diesel Engines
items.description: Diesel Golf engines dominate UK replacement enquiries, with the 1.6/2.0 TDI EA288 family covering Mk6-Mk8 variants and known for injector, DPF and timing chain failure patterns.
items.descriptor: Diesel Golf engines dominate UK replacement enquiries, with the 1.6/2.0 TDI EA288 family covering Mk6-Mk8 variants and known for injector, DPF and timing chain failure patterns.
items.familiesLabel: Common Volkswagen Golf Diesel Engine Families
families: CAYC/CRBC - 1.6 TDI (Mk6/Mk7), injector leak-off and DPF issues
CFHC/CUNA - 2.0 TDI 140/150hp (Mk6/Mk7), timing chain tensioner risk
CRLB - 2.0 TDI 184hp GTD (Mk7), twin-turbo oil seal and EGR failures
BKC - 1.9 TDI PD (Mk4/Mk5), robust but injector/cam wear at high mileage
knownFor: Strong motorway fuel economy and torque for loaded Golfs
Injector leak-off failures on 1.6 TDI (CAYC) between 70k-120k miles
DPF/EGR blockage on short-journey urban Golfs triggering limp mode
High UK replacement demand on Mk6/Mk7 2.0 TDI variants
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf Diesel replacement engines

items.title: Volkswagen Golf Petrol Engines
items.description: Petrol Golf engines span the efficient 1.0/1.2 TSI three-cylinders to the high-performance EA888 2.0 TSI in GTI/R variants, with timing chain and carbon buildup as key failure themes.
items.descriptor: Petrol Golf engines span the efficient 1.0/1.2 TSI three-cylinders to the high-performance EA888 2.0 TSI in GTI/R variants, with timing chain and carbon buildup as key failure themes.
items.familiesLabel: Common Volkswagen Golf Petrol Engine Families
families: CMBA/CZDA - 1.4 TSI EA211 (Mk7), timing belt tensioner and carbon buildup
CXCA/CXDA - 2.0 TSI EA888 Gen 3 (GTI), improved but still monitor chain tension
CJX - 2.0 TSI EA888 high-output (R), turbo bearing and oil feed considerations
DADA - 1.0 TSI (Mk8), HPFP and turbo reliability on early units
foundIn: Mk8 (2020-Present) - 1.0/1.5 TSI Evo, 2.0 TSI EA888 evo4
knownFor: Excellent responsiveness and tuning potential on TSI engines
EA111 timing chain stretch on early 1.4 TSI (pre-2013) requiring preventative replacement
Carbon buildup on direct injection TSI engines affecting idle and performance
EA888 Gen 2 timing chain tensioner failures on Mk6 GTI/R (pre-2013)
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf Petrol engine prices

items.title: Volkswagen Golf Mild Hybrid (MHEV) Engines
items.description: Mild-hybrid Golf variants (Mk8 onwards) use a 48V belt-driven starter-generator to assist the 1.0/1.5/2.0 TSI engines, improving economy without plug-in complexity.
items.descriptor: Mild-hybrid Golf variants (Mk8 onwards) use a 48V belt-driven starter-generator to assist the 1.0/1.5/2.0 TSI engines, improving economy without plug-in complexity.
families: DADA 1.0 TSI MHEV - entry-level Mk8 Golf
DJHA 1.5 TSI Evo MHEV - mainstream Mk8 Golf
DFHA 2.0 TDI MHEV - diesel Mk8 Golf
48V belt-driven starter-generator recovers braking energy and assists acceleration
Enables coasting with engine off at cruise, improving real-world fuel economy
Engine replacement requires hybrid system inspection and potential 48V battery health check
importantNotes: MHEV systems do not enable electric-only driving; engine still required for propulsion
Replacement engines must be matched to MHEV-compatible ECU and wiring harness
Specialist knowledge required for 48V system safety during engine removal/installation
items.cta: Enquire about Volkswagen Golf MHEV engine availability

items.title: Volkswagen Golf Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV) Engines
items.description: The GTE and eHybrid variants combine a 1.4L TSI petrol engine with an electric motor and high-voltage battery for electric-only urban driving and extended range capability.
items.descriptor: The GTE and eHybrid variants combine a 1.4L TSI petrol engine with an electric motor and high-voltage battery for electric-only urban driving and extended range capability.
families: 1.4L TSI petrol engine paired with electric motor and ~13kWh battery (GTE) or ~10kWh (eHybrid)
Electric-only range of 30-40 miles (GTE) or 25-35 miles (eHybrid) before petrol engine engages
Engine replacement requires high-voltage system isolation and specialist hybrid training
importantNotes: PHEV engine replacement is more complex and costly than standard petrol due to hybrid integration
Battery health significantly impacts replacement economics; request diagnostic report before proceeding
Limited specialist availability for PHEV engine replacements compared to standard Golfs
items.cta: Enquire about Volkswagen Golf PHEV engine availability

engineYears.h2: Volkswagen Golf Engines by Year - What Was Fitted & When
engineYears.intro: This timeline covers all major engine changes across the Volkswagen Golf's production history, organised so owners can quickly identify their engine and compare rebuilt engine prices from UK specialists.

years.preview: Mk5 Golf: PD Diesel & Early TSI Arrival
years.description: The Mk5 Golf introduced the new PQ35 platform and brought PD diesel technology alongside early TSI petrol engines to the UK range.
keyChanges: New PQ35 platform replacing Mk4 architecture
Introduction of 2.0 TDI PD (BKD) and 1.9 TDI PD (BKC) diesel engines
Early 1.4 TSI EA111 twincharger engine on select high-spec variants
DSG dual-clutch transmission becomes widely available
knownFor: Robust PD diesel engines with high-mileage potential when maintained
Early EA111 timing chain tensioner vulnerability on 1.4 TSI
DSG mechatronic unit faults emerging on high-mileage examples
Strong parts availability and specialist knowledge in UK market
years.cta: Get quotes for 2004-2009 Volkswagen Golf replacement engines
years.ticker: Volkswagen Golf 1.9 TDI engine replacement • Volkswagen Golf 2.0 TDI PD injector failure • Volkswagen Golf 1.4 TSI timing chain rattle
enquiries: Volkswagen Golf 2.0 TDI PD injector failure
Volkswagen Golf 1.4 TSI timing chain rattle

years.description: The Mk6 facelift introduced the EA288 diesel family and refined EA111 petrol engines, but also brought new failure modes around injectors and timing components.
keyChanges: Introduction of EA288 1.6/2.0 TDI diesel engines (CAYC, CFHC)
EA111 1.4 TSI becomes mainstream petrol option
Start-stop technology introduced on BlueMotion variants
Revised interior and exterior styling over Mk5
knownFor: CAYC injector leak-off failures driving high UK replacement demand
CFHC timing chain tensioner vulnerability requiring monitoring
EA111 timing chain stretch on early 1.4 TSI units
Strong economy from BlueMotion variants but DPF issues on short journeys
years.cta: Get quotes for 2009-2013 Volkswagen Golf replacement engines
years.ticker: Volkswagen Golf CAYC injector failure • Volkswagen Golf CFHC timing chain replacement • Volkswagen Golf 1.4 TSI engine rattle
enquiries: Volkswagen Golf CFHC timing chain replacement

years.description: The all-new MQB platform Mk7 Golf brought refined EA211 petrol and EA288 diesel engines, with significant reliability improvements but new failure patterns emerging.
keyChanges: New MQB platform with weight reduction and improved dynamics
EA211 petrol engines replace EA111 (timing belt vs chain architecture change)
EA888 Gen 3 2.0 TSI for GTI/R with improved timing components
Introduction of ACT cylinder deactivation on 1.4 TSI
knownFor: EA211 timing belt system more reliable than EA111 chain but still monitor tensioner
EA888 Gen 3 timing components improved but still vulnerable under hard use
CUNA/CRLB DPF/EGR issues on urban-use diesel Golfs
Strong performance and economy balance across the range
years.cta: Get quotes for 2013-2017 Volkswagen Golf replacement engines
years.ticker: Volkswagen Golf EA888 timing chain • Volkswagen Golf GTD DPF blockage • Volkswagen Golf 1.4 TSI ACT fault

years.preview: Mk7.5 Golf: EA211 Evo & EA888 Refinements
years.description: The Mk7.5 facelift introduced the 1.5 TSI Evo engine with improved efficiency and further EA888 Gen 3 refinements for GTI/R variants.
keyChanges: Introduction of 1.5 TSI Evo engine (DJHA) replacing 1.4 TSI
EA888 Gen 3B updates for GTI/R with further reliability improvements
Mild-hybrid technology introduced on select diesel variants
Updated infotainment and driver assistance systems
knownFor: 1.5 TSI Evo generally reliable but early units saw turbo/HPFP concerns
EA888 Gen 3B further reduced timing component failure risk
Mild-hybrid diesel variants improve economy but add system complexity
Strong enthusiast following for GTI/R performance variants
years.cta: Get quotes for 2017-2020 Volkswagen Golf replacement engines
years.ticker: Volkswagen Golf 1.5 TSI Evo turbo • Volkswagen Golf R CJX engine • Volkswagen Golf GTI CXDA replacement

years.preview: Present - Mk8 Golf: Digital Cockpit & Electrification Push
years.description: The all-digital Mk8 Golf emphasizes connectivity and electrification, with MHEV systems across the range and updated EA211 evo4/EA888 evo4 engines.
keyChanges: New digital cockpit and touch-sensitive controls
MHEV (48V mild-hybrid) standard on most petrol/diesel variants
EA211 evo4 and EA888 evo4 engine updates for emissions compliance
Introduction of eHybrid plug-in hybrid variant
knownFor: MHEV systems improve economy but add complexity to engine replacement
Early Mk8 software teething issues affecting engine management
Strong emphasis on electrification reducing pure combustion variant availability
High specialist knowledge requirement for MHEV/PHEV engine work
years.cta: Get quotes for 2020-Present Volkswagen Golf replacement engines
years.ticker: Volkswagen Golf Mk8 MHEV engine • Volkswagen Golf eHybrid battery • Volkswagen Golf GTI DLRA replacement

years.description: The iconic R32 Golfs featured the smooth 3.2L VR6 engine, while early TFSI performance variants laid groundwork for modern GTI/R engineering.
keyChanges: Introduction of 3.2L VR6 engine for R32 variants
Early 2.0 TFSI EA113 engines for GTI (pre-EA888)
Haldex 4Motion system on R32 and 4Motion variants
Distinctive styling and enthusiast following for R32 models
knownFor: VR6 engine renowned for smoothness and character but timing chain guide wear at high mileage
Early EA113 TFSI cam follower wear requiring monitoring
Strong enthusiast community and parts support for legacy performance variants
High replacement demand for R32 VR6 engines among collectors
years.cta: Get quotes for legacy Volkswagen Golf R32 & early performance engines
years.ticker: Volkswagen Golf R32 VR6 engine • Volkswagen Golf GTI AXX cam follower • Volkswagen Golf 1.9 TDI PD injector

faq.h2: Volkswagen Golf Engine Replacement - Frequently Asked Questions

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine on a Volkswagen Golf?
items.answer: Yes, if your Golf is in good overall condition and the replacement cost is less than 50-60% of the car's post-repair value. Rebuilt engines from £950 make this viable for most Mk6/Mk7 variants.
keyPoints: Rebuilt Golf engines typically cost £950-£3,800 supply-only
Supply & fit adds £600-£1,100 depending on workshop
Consider car's pre-failure value, mileage, and service history
A rebuilt engine with warranty often beats scrapping or part-exchange
items.cta: Compare rebuilt Volkswagen Golf engine prices for your variant

items.question: How much does it cost to replace a Volkswagen Golf engine?
items.answer: UK rebuilt Golf engine prices range from £950 for a 1.6 TDI to £4,800 for a Golf R EA888, plus £600-£1,100 for professional fitting if required.
keyPoints: Diesel engines: £950-£2,900 rebuilt supply-only
Petrol engines: £1,200-£3,800 rebuilt supply-only
Performance variants (GTI/R): £2,400-£4,800
Supply & fit labour typically adds £600-£1,100 nationwide
items.cta: Get a tailored Volkswagen Golf engine replacement quote

items.question: What engine code is in my Volkswagen Golf?
items.answer: Your Golf's engine code is stamped on the engine block (near the timing cover) and listed on your V5C logbook under "engine number" or "type approval".
keyPoints: Common codes: CAYC (1.6 TDI), CFHC (2.0 TDI), CXCA (2.0 TSI GTI)
Enter your registration on Engines Market for instant code lookup
Engine code determines compatible replacement options and pricing
Always verify code before ordering to avoid fitment issues
items.cta: Identify your Volkswagen Golf engine code instantly

items.question: Can I fit an Audi A3 or Seat Leon engine in my Golf?
items.answer: Yes, many Golf engines share platforms with Audi A3, Seat Leon, and Skoda Octavia, but ECU coding and mounting points require specialist verification before swapping.
keyPoints: EA288 diesels and EA888 petrols are often cross-compatible
ECU immobiliser coding may require dealer or specialist tools
Mounting brackets and ancillaries can differ between brands
Always confirm compatibility with your specific Golf variant first
items.cta: Check Volkswagen Golf engine compatibility with Audi/Seat units

items.question: How long should a Volkswagen Golf engine last?
items.answer: Well-maintained Golf engines regularly exceed 150,000-200,000 miles; diesel TDI units often outlast petrol TSI if serviced correctly and driven appropriately.
keyPoints: Regular oil changes with correct spec oil are critical
Timing component replacement at manufacturer intervals prevents catastrophic failure
Short-journey urban use accelerates DPF/EGR issues on diesels
High-mileage Golfs benefit from rebuilt engines with upgraded components
items.cta: Find long-life rebuilt Volkswagen Golf engines with warranty

items.question: What is the most reliable engine for a Volkswagen Golf?
items.answer: The 1.9 TDI PD (BKC) and later EA288 2.0 TDI (CUNA) are widely regarded as the most robust, while EA888 Gen 3+ petrol engines have significantly improved reliability over early versions.
keyPoints: 1.9 TDI PD: simple, robust, but injector wear at high mileage
EA288 2.0 TDI: strong torque, but monitor timing components
EA888 Gen 3+ petrol: much improved over Gen 2 timing issues
Avoid early EA111 1.4 TSI without timing chain preventative maintenance
items.cta: Compare reliable Volkswagen Golf engine options by code

items.question: Does a replaced Volkswagen Golf engine come with a warranty?
items.answer: Yes, all engines supplied through Engines Market include a minimum 12-month unlimited mileage warranty from UK specialists, with some offering 24-month coverage.
keyPoints: Warranty covers parts and labour for manufacturing/rebuild defects
Always confirm exact terms with your chosen supplier before ordering
Keep service records to maintain warranty validity
Supply & fit packages often include installation warranty too
items.cta: Find Volkswagen Golf engines with 12-month warranty coverage

items.question: Should I choose supply-only or supply & fit for my Golf engine?
items.answer: Supply-only saves money if you have a trusted independent garage; supply & fit offers convenience, warranty coverage on installation, and specialist Golf knowledge.
keyPoints: Supply-only: lower upfront cost, but you arrange fitting separately
Supply & fit: single point of contact, installation warranty included
Golf transverse engine layout requires specialist tools/knowledge
Compare both options via Engines Market to find best value
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf supply-only vs supply & fit engine deals

items.question: How much does timing chain replacement cost on a Volkswagen Golf?
items.answer: Preventative timing chain kit replacement costs £550-£950 at a specialist; if failure has caused interference damage, a rebuilt engine (£1,200-£3,800) is often more cost-effective.
keyPoints: EA111/EA888 timing chain kits: £550-£950 specialist fitted
Cylinder head repair after chain jump: £1,200-£2,300
Rebuilt engine with updated tensioner: £1,200-£3,800 supply-only
Preventative replacement at 80k miles avoids catastrophic failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Volkswagen Golf timing chain or rebuilt engine

items.question: What causes DPF problems on Volkswagen Golf diesel engines?
items.answer: Frequent short journeys prevent DPF regeneration, causing soot accumulation, restricted exhaust flow, and limp mode; urban-use Golfs are most vulnerable.
keyPoints: DPF requires sustained high-load driving to regenerate naturally
Short trips cause soot buildup triggering warning lights
Forced regeneration or professional cleaning may restore function
Rebuilt engines with DPF service address combined failure risks
items.cta: Compare Volkswagen Golf diesel engines with DPF service included
